coredata.dc:description: Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) is highly toxic, widely distributed, and difficult to monitor, posing a serious threat to human health. Therefore, a highly sensitive, rapid, convenient, and low-cost detection method must be urgently established. In this study, a triple strategy-enhanced immunochromatographic assay (ICA) was developed to satisfy these detection requirements. First, a turn-on signal output mode of the fluorescence quenching ICA substituted the turn-off mode of the traditional ICA for sensitive response to trace AFM1, with the limit of detection (LOD) reduced by approximately 4.9-fold. Then, a novel Au and polydopamine (PDA) cogrowth chrysanthemum-like blackbody was prepared as the quenching probe to reduce the background signal. This probe combined the excellent properties of Au nanoparticles with PDA. Thus, its fluorescence quenching constant was higher than that of single Au and PDA nanoparticles by 25.8- and 4.9-fold, respectively. Furthermore, an aggregation-induced emission fluorescence microsphere with a 5.7-fold higher relative quantum yield than a commercial fluorescence microsphere was selected as the signal output carrier to improve the signal-to-noise ratio. The integration of the above triple strategies established a 53.4-fold sensitivity-enhanced fluorescence quenching ICA (LOD = 0.9 pg/mL) for detecting AFM1 in milk, providing a strong technical guarantee for the safety monitoring of milk products.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of APCB, AuNP, and PDAN. TEM and HRTEM of (A, D) APCB, (B, E) AuNP, and (C, F); the insets are the corresponding signal particle TEM and photos (0.1 mg/mL), respectively. (G) XRD, (H) XPS, (I) O 1 s spectrum, (J) Au 4 f spectrum, and (K) zeta potential values of APCB, AuNP, and PDAN. (L) UVvis absorption spectroscopy (0.1 mg/mL) of APCB, AuNP, and PDAN. Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of AIEFM (0.1 mg/mL) and the corresponding photo (inset) taken under (left) natural light and (right) UV light. (M) Fluorescence spectroscopy of AIEFM (0.1 mg/mL) after adding different quenchers (0.5 mg/mL) (n = 3). Blank represents AIEFM without adding quencher.

Calculation of the absorption coefficient and fluorescence quenching constant. UV−vis absorption spectroscopy of (A) APCB, (B) AuNP, and (C) PDAN. The insets are corresponding photos (1–7: 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1 mg/mL). Absorption coefficient calibration curves of (D) APCB, (E) AuNP, and (F) PDAN. Stern−Volmer calibration curves of (G) APCB, (H) AuNP, and (I) PDAN (n = 3).

Evaluation of the stability and coupling efficiency of four nanoquenchers (APCB, AuNP, PDAN, and AuNP-70). Tolerance to (A) pH (3−10) and (B) different NaCl concentrations (0–1 M) (n = 3). The insets are the corresponding photos. (C) Coupling efficiency of the four nanoquenchers and mAb at different concentrations. The concentration of four nanoquenchers was 0.1 mg/mL. (D) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (1–F/F0) of four nanoquenchers coupled with different concentrations of mAb. F represents the fluorescence intensity of the T line sprayed with AIEFM after binding the probe (0.1 mg/mL). F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the T line sprayed with AIEFM (0.1 mg/mL).

The advantage of AIEFM. Dark field image of laser scanning confocal microscopy of (A) AIEFM (taken under 365 nm laser excitation), (B) FM1 (taken under 490 nm laser excitation), and (C) FM2 (taken under 440 nm laser excitation) by laser scanning confocal microscopy with 5.00% laser power and 1.00 AU pinhole. (D) Fluorescence emission spectrum, (E) UV–vis spectrum, and (F) histogram of I/A (integral of fluorescence intensity/absorbance) of AIEFM, FM1, and FM2. The concentrations of all of the FMs were 0.1 mg/mL. (G) Fluorescence constants of AIEFM, FM1, and FM2 on the NC membrane. (H) Effect of the concentration of AIEFM sprayed on the NC membrane on antigen-antibody binding efficiency. (I) Comparison of S/N when maintaining the close fluorescence intensity with different FMs (AIEFM: 0.1 mg/mL, FM1: 0.7 mg/mL, and FM2: 0.7 mg/mL) sprayed on the NC membrane. F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the empty FQICA test strip. F- and F+ represent the fluorescence intensity of the FQICA test strip after binding APCB-mAb in the negative sample (AFM1 = 0 ng/mL) and positive sample (AFM1 =0.5 ng/mL).

Triple strategy-enhanced ICA for ultrasensitive detection of AFM1. Detection of AFM1 based on (A) traditional AuNP-ICA, (B) AuNP-FM1-FQICA, (C) APCB-FM1-FQICA, and (D) APCB-AIEFM-FQICA (n = 3). (E) Comparison of the LOD of the above four assays. (F) Specificity experiment of APCB-AIEFM-FQICA (n = 3).

Triple strategy-enhanced immunochromatographic assay for ultrasensitive detection of AFM1. Strategy 1. Signal output mode – transform to turn on mode for sensitive response. Strategy 2. Quencher–using APCB with high quenching efficiency to reduce background signal. Strategy 3. Signal output–using AIEFM with high fluorescence efficiency to improve the signal of positive samples.
Molar absorption coefficient and fluorescence quenching constant of nanoquenchers.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) is highly toxic, widely distributed, and difficult to monitor, posing a serious threat to human health. Therefore, a highly sensitive, rapid, convenient, and low-cost detection method must be urgently established. In this study, a triple strategy-enhanced immunochromatographic assay (ICA) was developed to satisfy these detection requirements. First, a turn-on signal output mode of the fluorescence quenching ICA substituted the turn-off mode of the traditional ICA for sensitive response to trace AFM1, with the limit of detection (LOD) reduced by approximately 4.9-fold. Then, a novel Au and polydopamine (PDA) cogrowth chrysanthemum-like blackbody was prepared as the quenching probe to reduce the background signal. This probe combined the excellent properties of Au nanoparticles with PDA. Thus, its fluorescence quenching constant was higher than that of single Au and PDA nanoparticles by 25.8- and 4.9-fold, respectively. Furthermore, an aggregation-induced emission fluorescence microsphere with a 5.7-fold higher relative quantum yield than a commercial fluorescence microsphere was selected as the signal output carrier to improve the signal-to-noise ratio. The integration of the above triple strategies established a 53.4-fold sensitivity-enhanced fluorescence quenching ICA (LOD = 0.9 pg/mL) for detecting AFM1 in milk, providing a strong technical guarantee for the safety monitoring of milk products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1), the product of aflatoxin B1 metabolism in mammals, can stably exist in dairy products. The long-term intake of dairy products containing AFM1 can cause liver damage and even cancer in humans [1]. The European Commission [2] and the Ministry of Agriculture of China [3] set the strict maximum residue limit (MRL) of AFM1 in milk to 0.05 and 0.5 ng/mL, respectively, to safeguard human health. AFM1 is highly toxic, widely distributed, and difficult to monitor. Therefore, a highly sensitive, rapid, convenient, and low-cost detection method must be urgently established. At present, the widely used detection methods of AFM1 mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], liquid chromatographymass spectrometry (LCMS) [5], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6], and immunochromatographic assay (ICA) [7]. HPLC, LCMS, and ELISA provide high sensitivity. However, they are time-consuming and require professional operation. ICA has the advantages of rapidity, convenience, and low cost. However, the detection sensitivity is unsatisfactory and must be improved.

The traditional turn-off ICA that detects AFM1 adopts the principle of competitive inhibition [8]. AFM1 in the sample solution competes with the antigen sprayed on the test line (T line) of the ICA test strip for the antibody labeled on the signal probe. The detection signal intensity is inversely proportional to the concentration of AFM1. In the “turn off” mode, the signal response is too weak to trace AFM1, resulting in poor detection sensitivity [9,10]. The turn-on fluorescence quenching ICA (FQICA) [11] utilizes the fluorescent material mixed with the antigen sprayed on the T line as the detection output signal and the fluorescence nanoquencher as the probe to label antibodies. It has been recently reported as an effective strategy for improving sensitivity. In turn-on mode, the signal intensity is proportional to the target concentration, and the background signal is extremely low; thus, trace amounts of targets can cause obvious signal changes [12].

FQICA relies on nanoquenchers as probes to quench fluorescence on the T line efficiently, thereby ensuring an ultralow fluorescence background signal and limit of detection (LOD). At present, the commonly used nanoquenchers include some noble metal nanomaterials (e.g., Au [13] and Ag [14] nanoparticles) with plasmonic properties and nonmetal nanomaterials (e.g., polydopamine [PDA] [15] and carbon [16] nanoparticles) with super absorptive performance. However, the relatively narrow characteristic absorption peak of noble metal nanomaterials [17] only has a certain quenching effect on the emission (Em) light or excitation (Ex) light, resulting in low quenching efficiency. Nonmetal nanomaterials [18] mainly absorb the Ex light of fluorescent materials, and their absorption of the Em light is relatively weak. Thus, their quenching ability still needs to be improved. Therefore, one strategy is to improve the sensitivity of FQICA by enhancing the fluorescence quenching ability of the quencher further, in ways such as combining Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) possessing plasmonic properties with PDA possessing super absorptive performance.

At present, the widely used fluorescent materials of FQICA include fluorescein [19,20], carbon dots [21], gold clusters [22], quantum dots [23,24], and time-resolved fluorescent microspheres [25]. However, these traditional fluorescent materials have a low quantum yield (QY), weak fluorescence intensity, and serious aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [26] phenomenon when sprayed on the T line of the ICA test strip. These features, which limit the signal of positive samples, are not conducive to high-sensitivity detection. Therefore, using fluorescent materials with higher fluorescence intensity and photostability, such as aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [27] material, which has great application prospects in biosensors and bioimaging, is one of the strategies to improve the sensitivity of FQICA. In AIE materials, the higher the aggregation degree is, the stronger the fluorescence; thus, AIE materials are ideal fluorescent materials that are more suitable for ICA than traditional ACQ fluorescent materials [28].

In this study, the turn-off signal output mode of the traditional ICA was transformed to the turn-on mode for a sensitive response to trace AFM1. Then, a novel Au and PDA cogrowth chrysanthemum-like blackbody (APCB) was prepared which combined AuNPs with plasmonic properties and PDA with broad-spectrum absorption properties; thus, it exhibited better optical properties, stability, and biocompatibility than single AuNPs and PDA nanoparticles (PDANs). Therefore, the APCB with efficient quenching ability is used not only as a nanoquencher for reducing background interference but also as a label for efficiently conjugating antibodies. Furthermore, the AIE fluorescent microsphere (AIEFM) with excellent fluorescence properties was used as a signal output carrier to improve the signal of positive samples and signal-to-noise ratio (S/N). The above triple strategies ( Scheme 1) were integrated to establish an ultrasensitive, rapid, and convenient FQICA for AFM1 detection in milk, which provides a strong technical guarantee for the safety monitoring of milk and dairy products.

Tetramethyl 4′,4'',4‴,4''''-(ethene-1,1,2,2-tetrayl)tetrakis([1,1′-biphenyl]−4-carboxylate) (TCBPE), polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), poly(maleic anhydride-alt-1-octadecene) (PMAO), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), CHCl3, HAuCl4, sodium citrate (SC), hydroquinone, AFM1, AFB1, ochratoxin A (OTA), fumonisin (FB), zearalenone (ZEN), deoxynivalenol (DON), patulin (PAT), casein, and bovine serum albumin (BSA), and 3,3', 5,5"-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Merck KGaA, Germany). NaOH, HCl, Tris-HCl, dopamine hydrochloride (DA·HCl), polyvinyl pyrrolidone 10000 (PVP 10000), ethanol, NH3·H2O, KH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaCl, H2O2 (30 wt%), H2SO4 and KCl were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (China). Traditional fluorescent microspheres (FMs) (Ex/Em: 490/530 nm; Ex/Em: 440/490 nm; 10 mg/mL) were purchased from Ocean (Ocean Nanotech, US) and Estapor (Merck KGaA, Germany), respectively. In addition, anti-AFM1 monoclonal antibody (mAb) and antigen (AFM1-BSA) were prepared in our laboratory. Goat anti-mouse secondary antibody (sAb; Code #: 205–005–108) and HRP-labeled sAb (Code #: M32207) were supplied by Jackson Immuno Research Laboratories, Inc (Shanghai, China) and Thermo Fisher Scientific (China) Co., Ltd, respectively. The sample pad, conjugate pad, nitrocellulose (NC) membrane, and absorption pad were purchased from Millipore, Inc (Bedford, MA). All solvents and other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. Milk samples were purchased from Rainbow Supermarket in Jiangxi Province (Nanchang, China).

APCB was prepared by a one-pot growth method. Briefly, 6 mL of ethanol, 108 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.01 M, pH 8.5), and 3.6 mL of PVP1000 (2 mg/mL) was mixed evenly, then 7.2 mL of DA·HCl (4 mg/mL) and 7.2 mL of HAuCl4 (5 mM) were added into the solution in turn under vigorous stirring. Then the solution was stirred continuously for 20 h in dark after the color changing from pale yellow to black. Finally, the solution was centrifuged at 13,000 r/min for 15 min (4 °C). The supernatant was discarded, and the resultant pellet was redissolved in ultrapure water to obtain APCB solution.

AIEFM was prepared via the microemulsion method as described in our previous study [28] with some adjustments. First, 30 mg of TCBPE was mixed with 40 mg of PMMA and 30 mg of PMAO in 1 mL CHCl3. After dissolving fully, the above solution was added to 5 mL of SDS solution (2.5 mg/mL). In an ice bath, an emulsion was obtained by ultrasonication at a power of 150 W for 5 min. Subsequently, CHCl3 in the emulsion was evaporated using a rotary evaporator. The resulting water-soluble AIEFM was purified by centrifugation (10000 r/min, 30 min) and washed three times with NaOH (0.01 M) and ultrapure water, respectively. Finally, the AIEFM was dissolved to 10 mg/mL with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the molar absorption coefficient and fluorescence quenching constant
ce:section.ce:para: The molar absorption coefficient (ε) of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, and PDAN was measured as follows. First, ultravioletvisible (UVvis) absorption spectroscopy (300–800 nm) of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, and PDAN at different concentrations (0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.1 mg/mL) was measured. The linear regression equation was established to calculate the absorption coefficient. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the absorbance value at 365 or 530 nm as the ordinate (Y) and the concentrations of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, or PDAN as the abscissa (X). Then, the slope of the linear fit was used to calculate ε according to the Lamber Beer Law: A = εbC [29], where A is the absorbance value at 365 or 530 nm, b is the thickness of the liquid, C is the molarity of the nanoquencher, and ε is the molar absorption coefficient. The molarity (C) of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, and PDAN (1 mg/mL) was calculated according to the particle number concentration (Qn) measured with nanoparticle tracking analysis.

The fluorescence quenching constant (K) of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, and PDAN was measured as follows. First, a series of concentrations (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.1 mg/mL; 200 μL) of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, or PDAN were mixed with 20 μL of AIEFM solution (0.1 mg/mL). Then, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were measured after 5 min. A linear regression equation was established to calculate K. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting F0/F as the ordinate (Y) and the concentrations of APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, or PDAN as the abscissa (X). Then, the slope of the linear fit was used to calculate K according to the Stern–Volmer equation [30]: F0/F = 1 + KC. where F0 is the fluorescence intensity without the nanoquencher, F is the fluorescence intensity with the nanoquencher, K is the fluorescence quenching constant, and C is the molarity of the nanoquencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and coupling efficiency of nanoquenchers
ce:section.ce:para: APCB, AuNP, PDAN, and AuNP-70 solutions (200 μL, 0.1 mg/mL) were adjusted at different pH values (3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10) and 20 μL of different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 M) of NaCl solution was added. Then, the UVvis absorption spectra (300–800 nm) of the above four nanoquenchers were measured to evaluate stability.

The coupling efficiency of four nanoquenchers with mAb was evaluated as follows. First, 100 μL of different concentrations (0.05, 0.1, and 0.5 mg/mL) of mAb solution was added to 1 mL of four nanoquenchers (0.1 mg/mL) and incubated for 2 h. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 5000 r/min (4 °C) for 10 min and the supernatant was collected. Then, 100 μL of supernatant and a series of concentrations of mAb solution were added to the 96-well ELISA plate and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. After being washed three times, each well was blocked with 200 μL of blocking buffer at 37 °C for 2 h. After being washed three times, 100 μL of HRP-labeled sAb solution was added to each well. After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, the plates were washed three times and 100 μL of TMB substrate was added to react at 37 °C for 15 min. Then, the enzymatic reaction was stopped with 50 μL of H2SO4 (2 M). Finally, the absorbance at 450 nm (OD450) of the solution in the 96-well ELISA plate was measured. Each experiment was repeated three times. The OD450 of the supernatant was used to calculate the concentration of the unconjugated antibody with the established standard curve of a series of concentrations of mAb solution. The coupling efficiency was calculated by the following equation. Coupling efficiency = (total mAb − unconjugated mAb)/total mAb × 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the relative antigen–antibody binding efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The ICA test strips were prepared as follows. AFM1-BSA (0.3 mg/mL) was mixed with different concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 mg/mL) of the three FMs. Then, the mixed solution above and sAb (0.5 mg/mL) were sprayed onto the NC membrane as the T line and the control line (C line), respectively. The sprayed NC membrane was dried at 37 °C for 6 h. Finally, the sample pad, conjugate pad, NC membrane, and absorption pad were assembled as the test strip (Fig. S1).

The APCB-mAb was prepared as follows. APCB was diluted with 1 mL of boric acid (BB) buffer (0.02 M) at different pH levels (5.5, 6, 6.5, 7, and 7.5) and added with mAb (2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, and 4 μg). Then, the mixture was shaken (300 r/min) at 20 °C for 1 h, and 100 μL of 0.5 wt% casein was added and shaken (300 r/min) at 20 °C for 2 h. APCB-mAb was subsequently purified by centerfield (5000 r/min, 4 °C, 10 min) and resuspended in 100 μL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (0.01 M, pH 7.4). The preparation of AuNP-mAb was similar to the above steps.

The prepared APCB-mAb (2.5 μL) was added to 100 μL of PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4) and mixed evenly. The mixed solution was added onto the sample pad of test strips. After 15 min, a smartphone was used to take photos of the test strips. Then, the gray value of the T line via ImageJ was read. The relative antigen–antibody binding efficiency was calculated with the following equation. Binding efficiency = GFM / G0 × 100%, where GFM and G0 are the gray values of the T line sprayed with FMs and without FMs, respectively.

Three ICA test strips were prepared. Traditional ICA test strip: TheT line was sprayed with 0.3 mg/mL AFM1-BSA. FM1-FQICA test strip: The T line was sprayed with 0.3 mg/mL AFM1-BSA and 0.7 mg/mL FM1. AIEFM-FQICA test strip: The T line was sprayed with 0.3 mg/mL AFM1-BSA and 0.1 mg/mL AIEFM. The C line of three ICA test strips was sprayed with 0.5 mg/mL sAb. The remaining steps were the same as above.

AFM1 was spiked to different concentrations (0, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.0025, 0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2.5 ng/mL) with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4). Then, AuNP-mAb (3, 3.5, 4, 4.5, and 5 μL) or APCB-mAb (2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4, and 4.5 μL) solution was added to the spiked solution and gently shaken for 5 min. The mixed solution was added onto the sample pad of different test strips. After 15 min, the test strips were read for signal intensity with colloidal gold or a fluorescence reader. The linear regression equation was established for quantitative analysis. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the signal intensity as the ordinate (Y) and the concentrations of AFM1 as the abscissa (X). The limit of detection (LOD) of AFM1 was calculated by finding the concentration at which the signal-to-noise ratio was three (S/N = 3) [31].

Specificity experiments of APCB-AIEFM-FQICA were conducted as follows: PBS was spiked with AFB1, OTA, FB, ZEN, DON, and PAT at 5 ng/mL. AFM1 was spiked to 0.5 ng/mL. The spiked samples and PBS were detected using APCB-AIEFM-FQICA. The negative milk sample confirmed by LC–MS analysis was spiked with AFM1 to the concentrations of 0.2, 0.5, and 2.0 ng/mL. Then the spiked milk samples were diluted 100-fold with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4) and detected using APCB-AIEFM-FQICA. All experiments were conducted thrice.

Twenty actual milk samples obtained from the local supermarket were diluted 10-fold with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4). Then, the diluted samples were assessed with APCB-AIEFM-FQICA. A milk sample consisting of AFM1 was assessed to verify the accuracy of APCB-AIEFM-FQICA. Then, the milk sample consisting of AFM1 was diluted a series of multiples (100-fold, 200-fold, 400-fold, 800-fold, 1000-fold) with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4). Finally, the diluted samples were assessed with APCB-AIEFM-FQICA, AuNP-ICA and LCMS analysis to calculate the coefficient of determination (R2), which represented accuracy and precision. All experiments were conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of APCB, AuNP, and PDAN
ce:section.ce:para: APCB was synthesized via the one-pot cogrowth method, in which DA·HCl was used to reduce HAuCl4 and its oxidative self-polymerization product PDA was encapsulated in situ on the Au surface to form many highly branched petals. The TEM image ( Fig. 1A) showed that the average diameter of the APCB was 70.8 nm. Single AuNPs with average diameters of 23.1 nm (Fig. 1B) and 70.2 nm (AuNP-70, Fig. S2), and PDAN with an average diameter of 76.2 nm (Fig. 1C) were synthesized for comparison with the composite APCB. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1D shows that APCB is a polycrystalline structure with exposed crystal plane spacings of 0.20 and 0.23 nm, which is well associated with the characteristic (200) and (111) crystal faces of Au. However, only the Au (200) characteristic crystal face (0.20 nm) is shown in Fig. 1E, indicating that the AuNPs were a single crystal structure. No obvious lattice structure was observed in the HRTEM image of PDAN (Fig. 1F), showing that PDAN was an amorphous polymer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained to further investigate the crystalline properties (Fig. 1G). The diffraction peaks of AuNPs matched well with the standard spectra of Au (NO. 04–0784). PDAN exhibited a wide peak from 20° to 30°, confirming that PDAN was noncrystalline. APCB possessed both the syncretic wide peak from 20° to 30° and the characteristic peaks of Au, which was consistent with the HRTEM results.

Moreover, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to determine the chemical structure (Fig. 1H). The PDAN spectrum presented three peaks at 531.1, 398.5, and 284.1 eV corresponding to the characteristic peaks of O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively. In addition, the characteristic peak of Au 4 f (82.4 eV) was observed in the APCB spectrum, further suggesting the presence of Au, which accounts for 6.97%. Interestingly, the electron binding energy of the O 1 s (Fig. 1I) spectral peaks of PDAN was approximately 0.7 eV higher than that of APCB, demonstrating charge transfer from PDA to the Au surface. This phenomenon was most likely caused by the strong electron-donating capability of the functional groups along the polymer backbone. The Au 4 f spectrum (Fig. 1J) showed peaks at 83.6 and 87.3 eV, correlating to Au 4 f7/2 and Au 4 f5/2 binding, respectively, indicating that Au (0) was dominant in APCB. The electron binding energy of the Au 4 f spectral peak of APCB was 1.4 eV lower than that of AuNP, also indicating charge transfer from PDA to the Au surface. The reason was that Au with -OH of PDA forms an Au-O coordination bond in APCB, which was consistent with the O1s spectrum results. The characteristic N 1 s (Fig. S3A) peak at 399.8 eV belonged to pyrrolic N, which was the main form of the N element in both PDAN and APCB, indicating the sufficient oxidative self-polymerization of DA.

The uniform distribution of C, N, O and Au elements in the elemental mapping (Fig. S4) also confirmed that APCB was composed of Au and PDA. The hydration size distribution of APCB (Fig. S5) showed better monodispersity (particle distribution index, PDI = 0.054) than that of AuNP (PDI = 0.216) and approximate monodispersity with that of PDAN (PDI = 0.034). Fig. 1K demonstrated that APCB, AuNP, and PDAN have zeta potentials of − 38.4, − 21.7, and − 40.2 mV, respectively. The zeta potential of APCB (−38.4 mV) represented stronger electrostatic repulsion than that of AuNP (−21.7 mV) to ensure better dispersion stability, which illustrated that the existence of PDA improved the APCB dispersion. The optical properties of APCB, AuNP, and PDAN were also characterized. As shown in Fig. 1L, APCB possessed both the broad absorption peaks of PDAN and the characteristic absorption peak (at approximately 530 nm) of AuNPs, which largely overlapped with the fluorescence Ex and Em spectra of AIEFM. The strong and broad UV−vis absorption from 300 nm to 800 nm enabled the strong fluorescence quenching capacity of APCB. Compared with that after adding AuNPs and PDAN, the fluorescence intensity at the maximum Em wavelength (530 nm) of AIEFM after adding APCB was the lowest (Fig. 1M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mathematical relationship between fluorescence quenching constant and molar absorption coefficient of nanoquenchers
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching ability of nanoquenchers is usually positively correlated with their optical absorption ability. The strength of these two abilities is usually judged by the fluorescence quenching constant (K) and molar absorption coefficient (ε). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of AIEFM (4.6 ns) did not decay after APCB, AuNP, AuNP-70, and PDAN were added (Fig. S6). This finding indicated that the quenching mechanism of AIEFM was the inner filter effect, resulting from the absorption of the quencher to the Ex and Em light [32]. Therefore, the following assumption was made: K = aε + b (a and b are unknown constants).

The UVvis absorption spectra with different concentrations of APCB ( Fig. 2A), AuNP (Fig. 2B), AuNP-70 (Fig. S7A), and PDAN (Fig. 2C) were measured to establish their absorption coefficient calibration curves at 365 and 530 nm (maximum Ex and Em wavelengths of AIEFM). The large slope at 530 and 365 nm of APCB indicated its strong absorption ability to the Ex and Em light of AIEFM (Fig. 2D). The slope of AuNP (Fig. 2E) and AuNP-70 (Fig. S7B) at 530 nm was larger than that at 365 nm, which suggested that AuNP and AuNP-70 mainly absorb the Em light of AIEFM. In contrast, Fig. 2F shows that PDAN mainly absorbs the Ex light of AIEFM. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of AIEFM after the addition of different concentrations of quenchers (Figs. S7C and S8) were measured to establish the Stern–Volmer calibration curve of APCB (Fig. 2G), AuNP (Fig. 2H), AuNP-70 (Fig. S7D), and PDAN (Fig. 2I). Then, the ε and K of the above nanoquenchers were calculated and are listed in Table 1. The K of APCB (4.36 × 1011 L/mol) was 25.8-, 4.0- and 4.9-fold higher than that of AuNP, AuNP-70, and PDAN, respectively, indicating that the combination of AuNP and PDAN can obviously improve the fluorescence quenching ability of APCB. Finally, the mathematical relationship between K and ε of the quenchers was established (Fig. S9). K is proportional to ε365 and ε530. The linear regression equation could be presented as K = 1.8683ε365 + 0.50764ε530 − 2.093 × 1010 (R2 = 0.9955).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the stability and coupling efficiency of nanoquenchers
ce:section.ce:para: For applicability in FQICA, the stability and coupling efficiency of nanoquenchers were as important as the fluorescence quenching ability. Therefore, the tolerances to pH and NaCl were evaluated by collecting the UV−vis spectra of the four nanoquencher solutions adjusted at different pH values (Fig. S10) and added with different NaCl concentrations (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 3A, the absorbance at 530 nm (OD530) of APCB and PDAN was almost unchanged at pH values ranging from 3 to 7, whereas that of AuNP and AuNP-70 obviously decreased when the pH values were smaller than 5. Moreover, with increasing NaCl concentration, the OD530 of the four nanoquenchers also decreased because of nanoparticle aggregation. However, the OD530 of AuNPs and AuNP-70 decreased more remarkably than that of APCB and PDAN (Fig. 3B). These results suggested that APCB and PDAN had better tolerance to pH and NaCl than AuNPs and AuNP-70, which possibly results from the abundant surface functional groups (e.g., –NH2 and –OH) of PDA. Moreover, the UVvis absorption spectrum, zeta potential, average hydration size, and PDI of APCB were unchanged after 90 days at room temperature, indicating good long-term storage stability (Fig. S12).

The coupling efficiency of the four nanoquenchers was also evaluated, and APCB and PDAN showed higher coupling efficiency than AuNP and AuNP-70 (Fig. 3C). Even when the concentration of mAb increased to 0.5 mg/mL, the APCB and PDAN maintained high coupling efficiencies of 82.5% and 84.2%, respectively. In comparison, the coupling efficiency of AuNP and AuNP-70 decreased to 43.1% and 36.7%, respectively. Then, the probes prepared by coupling the above four nanoquenchers with mAb were applied to FQICA. APCB-mAb showed the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency (Fig. 3D), which can ensure a lower signal background of FQICA and is conducive to improving the detection sensitivity. In general, the combination of AuNPs and PDAN contributed to the good stability, high fluorescence quenching efficiency, and high mAb coupling efficiency of APCB. This finding suggested that APCB can be a better quenching probe than single AuNPs and PDAN applied in FQICA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the effect of fluorescent microspheres on the T line
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent material sprayed on the T line of the test strip was also a key of FQICA. Thus, the advantage of AIEFM was verified by comparison with two approximate size (average hydration sizes 200 nm, Fig. S13) of commercial traditional FMs (FM1 and FM2). The Ex and Em spectra of FM1 and FM2 are shown in Fig. S14. Fig. 4A shows that AIEFM emits strong green fluorescence under 365 nm laser excitation, whereas the fluorescence of FM1 (Fig. 4B) and FM2 (Fig. 4C) at the same concentration was obviously weaker than that of AIEFM. The fluorescence intensity of AIEFM (Ex = 365 nm, Em = 530 nm) was 4.4- and 6.4-fold higher than that of FM1 (Ex = 490 nm, Em = 530 nm) and FM2 (Ex = 440 nm, Em = 490 nm), respectively (Fig. 4D). Then, the UV–vis spectra of the above three FMs (Fig. 4E) were measured to calculate their relative QY according to the following equation: QYAIEFM/QYFM = (IAIEFM/AAIEFM)/(IFM/AFM) [33]. As shown in Fig. 4F, the relative QY of AIEFM was approximately 5.1- and 5.7-fold higher than that of FM1 and FM2, respectively. In a dilute solution, the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the concentration of FM according to the following equation: F = kC. The fluorescence scale coefficient (k = 33.568) of AIEFM in aqueous solution was approximately 3.9- and 4.8-fold higher than that of FM1 (k = 8.5174) and FM2 (k = 7.0106), respectively (Fig. S15). When the three FMs were sprayed on the NC membrane of the test strip (Fig. 4G), AIEFM also showed a fluorescence scale coefficient (k = 46128) larger than that of FM1 (k = 8975) and FM2 (k = 8025.4).

Then, the effects of the three FMs on antigen–antibody binding were explored. The gray value of the T line decreased with the increase in the concentration of FMs sprayed on the T line of the test strip, indicating that the probe (APCB-mAb) bound to the T line decreased (Fig. S16). The reason is that the presence of FMs may cover the binding sites of antigens, hindering the binding of antibodies on the probe with antigens on the T line. Compared with the relative antigen–antibody binding efficiency when no FMs were sprayed on the T line of the test strip, that when 0.5 mg/mL AIEFM, FM1, and FM2 were sprayed decreased to 86.7%, 77.8%, and 79.1%, respectively (Fig. 4H and S17). Compared with the fluorescence signal intensity of the NC membrane sprayed with the low concentration (0.1 mg/mL) of FMs, that of the positive sample of the FQICA test strip sprayed with FM1 (824) and FM2 (732) was too weak to distinguish positive and negative samples (Fig. S18). The required spraying concentrations of both FM1 and FM2 (0.7 mg/mL) were seven times higher than that of AIEFM (0.1 mg/mL) to maintain a close fluorescence intensity of the T line on the prepared FQICA test strips (Fig. 4I). This approach can decrease the relative antigen–antibody binding efficiency, resulting in a higher background signal and lower S/N of the FQICA test stirp spray with FM1 and FM2 than AIEFM. Moreover, both AIEFM (Fig. S19) and AIEFM-FQICA (Fig. S20) test strips exhibited good long-term storage stability, with the fluorescence intensities of 95.5% and 91.9% after 90 days, respectively. These results indicated that AIEFM with excellent fluorescence properties was more suitable as a signal output of FQICA for improving the signal intensity of positive samples and S/N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AFM1 based on APCB-FQICA
ce:section.ce:para: Two probes (APCB-mAb and AuNP-mAb) were prepared by coupling mAb to the surface of APCB and AuNP through electrostatic adsorption. As shown in Fig. S21, the average hydration size of APCB (78.8 nm, Fig. S5A) and AuNP (24.4 nm, Fig. S5B) with mAb coupling increased to 91.3 and 33.5 nm, respectively. The reduced zeta potential values of APCB-mAb (−24.8 mV) and AuNP-mAb (−9.1 mV) also suggested successful preparation. The pH, mAb amounts, and probe volume were optimized. For AuNP-ICA and AuNP-FM1-FQICA (Fig. S22), a pH of 6, 4 μg of mAb, and 4.5 μL of AuNP-mAb were the optimal assay conditions. For APCB-FM1-FQICA and APCB-AIEFM-FQICA (Fig. S23), pH values of 6.5, 2.5 μg of mAb, and 4 μL or 2.5 μL of APCB-mAb were the optimal assay conditions.

Then, the two probes were used to detect AFM1 in serial spiked samples and corresponding calibration curves ( Fig. 5) and photos (Fig. S24) were obtained. The linear range, LOD, linear fitting equation, and R2 was listed in Table S1. The LOD of AFM1 detection was calculated as 48.1 pg/mL based on traditional AuNP-ICA. The LOD of AuNP-FM1-FQICA (9.8 pg/mL) decreased by approximately 4.9-fold by transforming the signal output mode from turn-off to turn-on. Furthermore, instead of AuNPs, APCB with higher quenching efficiency was used as a probe to establish APCB-FM1-FQICA, of which the LOD (2.2 pg/mL) decreased approximately 4.5-fold. Finally, AIEFM, with better fluorescence properties, replaced FM1 as a signal output. The LOD of APCB-AIEFM-FQICA enhanced via the above triple strategies was reduced to 0.9 pg/mL (Fig. 5E), of which the sensitivity improved by 53.4-fold compared with that of traditional AuNP-ICA. The widely used methods to detect AFM1 mainly include HPLC, LCMS, and ELISA. All of these methods have the disadvantages of being time-consuming, having high detection costs, and requiring professional operators and unportable instruments (Table S2). The newly developed method (APCB-AIEFM-FQICA) not only overcomes these disadvantages, but also has higher detection sensitivity than other ICAs, regardless of whether a colorimetric probe (e.g., AuNP), fluorescent probe (e.g., quantum dot, FM, and time-resolved FM), and novel probe (e.g., immunomagnetic nanobeads and (PEI/PSS)4 red silica nanoparticles) were used or a new signal output mode (e.g., surface enhanced Raman scattering and ratio fluorescence) was adopted ( Table 2).

The results of the specificity experiment are shown in Fig. 5F. APCB-AIEFM-FQICA provided positive results with AFM1 (0.5 ng/mL) and AFB1 (5 ng/mL), and consistent negative results with five other mycotoxins at 5 ng/mL, indicating that the proposed APCB-AIEFM-FQICA had good specificity for AFM1 detection, except for a cross-reaction (48.6%) with AFB1 (5 ng/mL). Furthermore, the resist disturbance experiment showed that the APCB-AIEFM-FQICA impurities did not affect the detection of AFM1 (Fig. S25) except for AFB1. The reason was that AFM1, the hydroxylated metabolite of AFB1 in vivo, exhibited a very similar structure to AFB1. However, this finding did not affect the detection of AFM1 because AFB1 would not be nonexistent in milk samples.

A recovery experiment was performed to verify the accuracy of APCB-AIEFM-FQICA. As shown in Table S4, the average recoveries of AFM1 in spiked milk samples ranged from 88.0% to 106.5%, which was an acceptable range. In addition, a total of 20 actual milk samples purchased from a local supermarket were tested by APCB-AIEFM-FQICA to evaluate the on-site detection capability. As shown in Table S5, all milk samples met the requirements of the Chinese National Food Safety Standard MRL (0.5 ng/mL) for AFM1 in milk. The AFM1 residue of 14 milk samples was less than 0.05 ng/mL, meeting the requirements of the European Union. The test results based on APCB-AIEFM-FQICA were highly reliable (coefficient of variation <8.63%), suggesting that the proposed APCB-AIEFM-FQICA was promising in the actual detection of AFM1 in milk. Finally, a milk sample consisting of AFM1 was assessed to verify the accuracy of APCB-AIEFM-FQICA. Fig. S26 showed good correlations (R2 = 0.9979 or 0.9944) between the present method and AuNP-ICA or LC–MS.

In summary, a triple strategy-enhanced ICA (APCB-AIEFM-FQICA) was established for ultrasensitive AFM1 detection in milk. APCB, with excellent fluorescence quenching ability and high mAb coupling efficiency, was synthesized and applied to the FQICA strip as the quenching probe. The results proved that the APCB had better optical properties, stability, and biocompatibility than the single AuNPs and PDAN because of the combined effect of Au and PDA. Moreover, AIEFM was prepared as the signal output carrier. Its fluorescence performance was better than that of commercial traditional FMs. The integration of a favorable turn-on signal output mode, an efficient quenching probe–APCB, and an excellent fluorescent signal output carrier–AIEFM considerably enhanced the detection performance of traditional ICA. The proposed APCB-AIEFM-FQICA could detect AFM1 as low as 0.9 pg/mL, which was 53.4-fold lower than the LOD (48.1 pg/mL) of AuNP-ICA. The successful establishment of the assay confirmed that APCB and AIEFM applied to FQICA could further improve detection sensitivity, thereby enabling ultrasensitive food safety detection.

Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1), the product of aflatoxin B1 metabolism in mammals, can stably exist in dairy products. The long-term intake of dairy products containing AFM1 can cause liver damage and even cancer in humans. AFM1 is highly toxic, widely distributed, and difficult to monitor, posing a serious threat to human health. Here, a 53.4-fold sensitivity-enhanced fluorescence quenching immunochromatographic assay for detecting AFM1 was developed by integrating a favorable turn-on signal output mode, an efficient quenching probe–APCB, and an excellent fluorescent signal output–AIEFM, providing a sensitive method for safety monitoring of hazardous materials in the environment.
